links.rationale: Both ask the student to refer to specific passages or quotations in a text to support an idea or answer; cross-grade (GA 5 to OH 6) because GA's grade 6-8 evidence elements shift to locating evidence across sources with bibliographic recording, and GA never restates the inference half.

Same shared skill of citing specific passages from a text to support an idea or answer; cross-grade (GA 5 to OH 6), and GA frames it inside source curation rather than as a reading-analysis standard, so the inference half is uncovered.
Both require determining a theme and explaining how the text's particular details convey it, though GA narrows the mechanism to character relationships and omits Ohio's objective-summary requirement.
Both require analyzing how a theme is developed and expressed across a text through characters, events, and plot; GA adds cross-text comparison and has no objective-summary counterpart.
Both ask the student to analyze how a theme is conveyed and developed through its relationship to characters, events/plot, and structure; only Ohio's summary sub-standard is uncovered.
Both ask how narrative technique across the whole text develops plot and characters, matching Ohio's plot-unfolds-in-episodes framing.
Both ask the student to analyze how events, conflict, and characterization drive the plot forward toward resolution.
Both require analyzing how narrative elements/techniques work across a text to develop plot, characters, and setting.

GA's 'analyze how setting, events, conflict, and characterization influence plot' is nearly Ohio's 'how setting shapes the characters or plot' example; cross-grade (GA 6 to OH 7) because GA moves to narrative devices at grade 7.
Both ask how specific narrative moves across the text reveal character and develop plot, though GA does not single out dialogue or incidents as the unit of analysis.
Both require determining how figurative and connotative language choices contribute to meaning and tone; GA's 'wide variety of texts' scope covers literature, so no strand crossing.
Both require explaining how figurative and connotative language choices contribute to meaning, mood, or tone in a text.
Ohio's clause on rhyme and repetitions of sound within a verse or stanza matches GA's analysis of poetic sound devices and stanza structure.
Both require analyzing the impact of figurative, connotative, and literary-device word choices on meaning and tone; GA frames the impact as achieving purpose for an audience.

Both ask how a text's organizational structure conveys meaning, though Ohio's part-to-whole framing (how one scene or stanza fits the overall structure) is narrower than GA's author-modifies-structure framing.
Both require analyzing how an author's structural choices contribute to a text's meaning; GA does not name drama or poem forms specifically.
Ohio's analysis of how a poem's form contributes to meaning matches GA's analysis of poetic techniques including stanza and rhyme scheme.
Both require analyzing how differing organizational structures contribute to meaning and style; GA does not require the two-text comparison Ohio does.

Both ask the student to explain how a narrator's or speaker's perspective is developed and shapes the text; cross-grade (GA 5 to OH 6) because GA's grade 6 element requires two or more competing perspectives, which Ohio does not introduce until grade 7.
Both require analyzing how a single text develops and contrasts two or more perspectives of different narrators or characters; cross-grade (GA 6 to OH 7).

Both ask how narrative devices produce a calculated effect such as suspense or humor on the audience; GA does not name the point-of-view gap (dramatic irony) as the mechanism, so only the effect-analysis skill is shared.
Near-verbatim: both require comparing and contrasting literary texts in different forms or genres in terms of their approaches to similar themes and topics.
Near-verbatim: both require comparing and contrasting a fictional portrayal of a time, place, or character with a historical account of the same period.
Near-verbatim: both require analyzing how a modern literary work draws on themes, patterns of events, or character types from earlier material.

Both require identifying the main/central idea of an expository text and the facts and key details that convey it; GA frames this as recognizing expository techniques and has no objective-summary counterpart.
Both require analyzing multiple main ideas and the details that develop them across an expository text; GA has no objective-summary counterpart.
Both require analyzing the development of a central idea over the course of an expository text and its relationship to supporting facts and details; GA has no objective-summary counterpart.

Shared attention to figurative and connotative language in texts, but Ohio's grade 6 standard stops at determining word meaning while GA requires determining impact on meaning, mood, or tone; Ohio's technical-meaning clause has no GA counterpart.
Both require explaining how a specific figurative or connotative word choice contributes to meaning and tone; GA's 'wide variety of texts' scope covers informational text, so no strand crossing.
Both require analyzing the impact of specific word choices, including figurative and connotative language, on meaning and tone; GA's scope is text-type-neutral.
Both ask how organizational structure and features convey meaning and develop ideas; Ohio's part-to-whole framing is narrower than GA's author-modifies-structure framing.
Both require analyzing the structure an author uses to organize a text and how that structure develops the ideas and achieves the author's purpose.
Both require analyzing how structural choices develop and refine a text's ideas; GA does not narrow the unit of analysis to a single paragraph as Ohio does.
Both require determining an author's purpose in a text and explaining how the text develops it; GA additionally requires handling multiple purposes and audience targeting.
Both require explaining how an author's perspective is conveyed in the text; GA specifies the author's choice of evidence as the mechanism, which is one of the means Ohio leaves open.
Near-verbatim: both require determining the author's perspective in a text and analyzing how the author distinguishes that position from others.
Covers the 'purpose' half of Ohio's perspective-or-purpose standard: both require analyzing an author's purpose and how the text develops it for an audience.
Covers the 'purpose' half of Ohio's standard: both require determining an author's purpose in a text and analyzing how it is developed for specific audiences.

Ohio's demand that students analyze how an author acknowledges and responds to conflicting evidence or viewpoints is the same skill as GA's analysis of an identified counterclaim and rebuttal within an argument.

Both require using information carried by non-prose modes and text features alongside words to build a coherent understanding of a topic; GA does not frame it as integrating across separate media versions.
Both require tracing an argument's claim and supporting evidence and judging which claims the evidence actually supports.
Both require analyzing an argument's claim, counterclaim, and conclusion and assessing whether the evidence is relevant and the reasoning logically follows.
Both require delineating an argument's claim, evidence, counterclaim, and rebuttal and evaluating whether the evidence is relevant, logical, and sufficient.
Near-verbatim: both require comparing and contrasting one author's presentation of events with another author's on the same event or topic.
Verbatim: both require analyzing how two or more authors on the same topic shape their presentations by emphasizing different evidence or advancing different interpretations of facts.
Both require analyzing and evaluating where two or more texts on the same topic diverge on matters of evidence, fact, or interpretation.
Both require comparing conflicting perspectives on a single topic across multiple texts and analyzing how each text establishes its position.

unmatched.why: GA's grade 6-8 evidence elements (7.T.RA.2a) are about locating evidence across sources and recording bibliographic information for research, not citing textual evidence to support analysis and inference of a literary text.
GA's 8.T.RA.2a shifts entirely to locating source evidence under MLA documentation rules; nothing asks students to select the evidence that most strongly supports an analysis.
Same gap as RL.7.1 — GA relocates evidence use into Research & Analysis source-curation and citation mechanics rather than a reading-analysis standard.
Same gap as RL.8.1 — GA's grade 8 evidence element is MLA source documentation, not evaluative selection of textual evidence supporting an analysis.

GA's individuals/events/ideas element (5.T.T.2b) asks for relationships and interactions among several, not Ohio's skill of analyzing how one key individual, event, or idea is introduced, illustrated, and elaborated; the shared nouns are topic overlap, not skill overlap.
GA's only matching element, 5.T.T.2b (interactions between individuals, events, and ideas), ends at grade 5 and is two grades below Ohio 7 — out of the +/-1 range.
GA has no grade 7-8 element on how a text makes connections among and distinctions between individuals, ideas, or events; the progression ended at GA grade 5.

GA has no element on comparing the experience of reading a text to an audio, video, or live version; its multimodal work is (C) construction or (I) use of mode features to aid comprehension, neither of which is cross-medium comparison.
No GA element asks students to compare a written story, drama, or poem to its filmed, staged, or multimedia version or to analyze medium-specific techniques.
No GA element covers evaluating how faithfully a production adapts a text or judging director and actor choices.
No GA element asks students to compare a text to an audio, video, or multimedia version and analyze each medium's portrayal of the subject.
No GA (I) element in grades 7-8 asks students to evaluate the advantages and disadvantages of different mediums for presenting a topic; GA's closest element (5.T.C.1b) is three grades away.
GA's 2023 Texts domain has no text-complexity-band or independent-reading-proficiency standard, and no element covers activating prior knowledge to make text-to-self/text/world connections.
Same as RL.6.10 — GA has no grade-level text-complexity-band expectation or reader-side background-knowledge/connections element.
Same as RL.6.10 — GA has no text-complexity-band standard; 6.T.C.2c and its grade 7-8 twins concern background information's impact on the text's development, not the reader's prior knowledge.
GA has no text-complexity-band or literary-nonfiction proficiency standard anywhere in the Texts domain.
Same as RI.6.10 — no GA counterpart to a year-end complexity-band reading expectation.
Same as RI.6.10 — no GA counterpart to independent proficient reading at the high end of a complexity band.
